name: Health Check Setup
description: Configure container health checks so Docker can automatically detect unhealthy containers and orchestrators can restart them.

steps.title: Add HEALTHCHECK to a Dockerfile
steps.description: Define a health check command that Docker runs periodically to determine if the container is healthy.
steps.tip: Adjust interval and timeout based on your application's startup time and response characteristics.

steps.title: Add health check in Docker Compose
steps.description: Configure health checks in your compose file for each service.
steps.command:
cat <<'EOF'
services:
  api:
    image: myapp:latest
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:3000/health"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 40s
EOF

steps.title: Check container health status
steps.description: View the health status and recent health check results.

steps.title: View health check history
steps.description: See the last few health check results including timestamps and output.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Health Check Setup and Verification
set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Container Health Status ==="
docker ps --format 'table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}'

echo ""
echo "=== Detailed Health for All Containers ==="
for container in $(docker ps -q); do
  name=$(docker inspect "$container" --format '{{.Name}}' | sed 's/\///')
  health=$(docker inspect "$container" --format '{{if .State.Health}}{{.State.Health.Status}}{{else}}no healthcheck{{end}}')
  echo "$name: $health"
done

faq.question: What is a good health check endpoint?
faq.answer: A /health endpoint that returns 200 OK when the app is ready to serve traffic. It should check critical dependencies (database, cache) but not be too expensive to run every 30 seconds.

faq.question: What does start_period do?
faq.answer: start_period gives the container time to initialize before health checks count. Failed checks during this period do not count toward the retry limit.

faq.question: How do I use health checks without curl?
faq.answer: Use 'wget -qO- http://localhost:3000/health' or for non-HTTP checks, write a small script that tests the service (e.g., pg_isready for PostgreSQL).
